data.title: Humana Cancer Care Tie Up With Atlas Oncology Reshapes Medicare Story
data.description:

  • The collaboration focuses on care coordination, patient support, and quality of life for oncology patients in Humana’s Medicare Advantage plans.
  • The new model aims to address both clinical and psychosocial needs for Medicare beneficiaries in covered regions.

Humana, trading at around $266.5 per share, has seen mixed share price performance, including a 2.5% decline over the past week and a 2.8% gain over the past month. Longer term, returns have been weaker, with 1 year, 3 year, and 5 year returns at 8.3%, 42.8%, and 27.2% declines respectively. In that context, this partnership illustrates how the company is working to refine its Medicare Advantage offering for complex, high-cost conditions such as cancer.

For investors following NYSE:HUM, the Atlas Oncology partnership is an operational development to watch, particularly around care quality metrics and member experience in oncology. As details emerge on rollout, uptake, and outcomes for Medicare Advantage members, you may gain a clearer view of how this approach to multidisciplinary cancer care fits into Humana’s broader Medicare strategy and capital allocation priorities.

How Humana stacks up against its biggest competitors

This partnership leans into one of Humana's core areas, Medicare Advantage, by trying to make cancer care more coordinated and supportive for members in Tennessee and Mississippi. By embedding interdisciplinary care teams, offering virtual urgent and palliative care, and tightening communication between oncologists, primary care doctors and other specialists, Humana is tying member experience more closely to integrated clinical pathways, which can be important for both quality scores and cost management in high acuity conditions.

data.title: Is RH (RH) A Potential Opportunity After A 45% One-Year Share Price Decline
data.description:
• If you are trying to figure out whether RH at around US$228 per share looks like a bargain or a value trap, it helps to first step back and look at what the recent share price has been telling you.
  • The stock has seen mixed returns, with a 1.9% decline over the last 7 days, a 24.8% gain over the last 30 days, an 18.1% gain year to date, but also a 45.5% decline over 1 year, a 26.5% decline over 3 years and a 52.0% decline over 5 years.
  • These swings suggest sentiment around RH has shifted more than once, and recent news coverage has focused on how investors are reassessing the business model, capital allocation and growth ambitions. Headlines have tended to link the share price moves to changing expectations about how the brand positions itself in the higher end home furnishings market and how that could affect longer term returns.
  • Simply Wall St currently gives RH a valuation score of 2 out of 6. This means it screens as undervalued on 2 of the 6 checks used in that model. Next, we will walk through the usual valuation tools investors lean on, then finish with a different way of thinking about value that might give you a clearer overall picture.

RH scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: RH Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today’s dollars. It is essentially asking what RH’s future cash generation might be worth to you right now.

RH’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about $76.4 million. The current model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, combining analyst estimates for the early years with longer term projections provided by Simply Wall St. For example, free cash flow is projected at $252.8 million in 2026 and $300.5 million in 2028, with further values extrapolated out to 2035. All these projected cash flows are discounted back to today using the DCF framework.

On this basis, the estimated intrinsic value comes out at about $404.21 per share, compared with a recent share price of roughly $228. That implies an intrinsic discount of around 43.5%, which indicates the shares screen as undervalued on this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests RH is undervalued by 43.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 864 more undervalued stocks based on cash flows.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for RH.

Approach 2: RH Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like RH, the P/E ratio is a common way to think about value because it links what you pay for the stock to the earnings the company is currently generating. The higher the expected growth and the lower the perceived risk, the more investors are usually willing to pay in terms of a higher P/E multiple.

RH currently trades on a P/E of 39.0x. That sits above both the Specialty Retail industry average P/E of 19.9x and a peer average of 18.4x, so on simple comparisons the shares look more expensive than many competitors.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for RH is 31.0x. This is a proprietary estimate of what a more “normal” P/E for the company could look like once you factor in its earnings growth profile, profit margins, risk characteristics, industry group and market cap together, rather than just lining it up against a crude sector average.

Because this Fair Ratio of 31.0x is below the current 39.0x P/E by a clear margin, RH screens as overvalued on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today’s value. This gives an estimate of what the whole business might be worth per share.

data.title: Hankyu Hanshin REIT (TSE:8977) Semi Annual Revenue Growth Challenges Bearish Narratives
data.description:
Hankyu Hanshin REIT (TSE:8977) has laid out its FY 2025 scorecard with second half total revenue of ¥6.5 billion and basic EPS of ¥3,392, alongside trailing twelve month totals of ¥12.9 billion in revenue and ¥6,894 in EPS that sit against a one year earnings growth rate of 12%. Over the past few reporting periods, total revenue has moved from ¥6.2 billion and EPS of ¥3,155 in the second half of FY 2024 to ¥6.4 billion and ¥3,503 in the first half of FY 2025, before landing at the latest ¥6.5 billion and ¥3,392 figures. TTM earnings growth outpaces 5 year trend

  • Trailing twelve month EPS is ¥6,894, compared with a 5 year earnings growth rate of 1.7% per year and a one year gain of 12%. The latest period therefore sits well above the long run pace.
  • What stands out for a more bullish angle is that this 12% one year earnings growth comes alongside higher net income of ¥4,793 million over the trailing twelve months, versus ¥4,280 million a year earlier. This strongly supports the idea of improving profit quality but also raises the question of how that squares with a dividend that is not well covered by earnings or free cash flow.
    • Supporters can point to the higher net profit margin of 37.1% compared with 35.2% last year as evidence that the recent EPS step up is backed by stronger profitability, not just accounting noise.
    • Cautious investors, however, may look at the same 3.84% dividend yield and weak coverage and argue that even with better margins, distributions could still be sensitive if earnings growth slows from this faster 12% clip back toward the older 1.7% trend.

To see how that growth story lines up with different long term views, check out how analysts weigh the positives and risks around Hankyu Hanshin REIT in the full narrative. 📊 Read the full Hankyu Hanshin REIT Consensus Narrative.

Margins and semi annual mix shift

  • Within FY 2025, first half net income of ¥2,435 million and EPS of ¥3,503 eased to ¥2,358 million and EPS of ¥3,392 in the second half, even though revenue moved from ¥6,417 million to ¥6,504 million across the same periods.
  • Critics highlight that this pattern, alongside a 37.1% net profit margin over the last 12 months compared with 35.2% the year before, challenges a pure bearish view that higher revenue inevitably comes at the expense of profitability, because the full year margin still improved even though the second half EPS was a touch below the first half.
    • Bears who focus on pressure points can still point to the softer second half EPS versus the first half as a sign that costs or property level drivers might be leaning against revenue at the margin.
    • At the same time, the trailing twelve month step up in net income from ¥4,280 million to ¥4,793 million suggests that when you zoom out from one half to the full trailing year, overall profit levels have not been squeezed in the way a more pessimistic story might suggest.

Mixed signals on yield and valuation

A DCF fair value of ¥4,483,593.88 and a 3.84% dividend yield sit in the background, even though that dividend is flagged as not well covered by earnings or free cash flow and the REIT is carrying a high level of debt.

Yet those concerns sit alongside a DCF fair value that is far above the current ¥164,000 price and a recent 12% earnings growth rate, which complicates any simple claim that the units are clearly expensive.
    • On one side, the combination of high debt and a dividend that lacks strong coverage gives bears concrete balance sheet and payout metrics to focus on if operating conditions change.
    • On the other, the improved net profit margin to 37.1% and higher trailing net income of ¥4,793 million versus ¥4,280 million limit the argument that profitability is already under clear strain at today’s valuation multiples.

data.title: Here's What We Like About Besterra's (TSE:1433) Upcoming Dividend
data.description:
Regular readers will know that we love our dividends at Simply Wall St, which is why it's exciting to see Besterra Co., Ltd. (TSE:1433) is about to trade ex-dividend in the next three days. The ex-dividend date is two business days before a company's record date in most cases, which is the date on which the company determines which shareholders are entitled to receive a dividend. The ex-dividend date is of consequence because whenever a stock is bought or sold, the trade can take two business days or more to settle. Accordingly, Besterra investors that purchase the stock on or after the 29th of January will not receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 8th of April.

The company's next dividend payment will be JP¥25.00 per share. Last year, in total, the company distributed JP¥30.00 to shareholders. Based on the last year's worth of payments, Besterra has a trailing yield of 2.2% on the current stock price of JP¥1350.00. Dividends are an important source of income to many shareholders, but the health of the business is crucial to maintaining those dividends. That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Dividends are usually paid out of company profits, so if a company pays out more than it earned then its dividend is usually at greater risk of being cut. Fortunately Besterra's payout ratio is modest, at just 36% of profit. That said, even highly profitable companies sometimes might not generate enough cash to pay the dividend, which is why we should always check if the dividend is covered by cash flow. It paid out 23% of its free cash flow as dividends last year, which is conservatively low.

It's positive to see that Besterra's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

See our latest analysis for Besterra

Click here to see how much of its profit Besterra paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

Companies with consistently growing earnings per share generally make the best dividend stocks, as they usually find it easier to grow dividends per share. If earnings decline and the company is forced to cut its dividend, investors could watch the value of their investment go up in smoke. That's why it's comforting to see Besterra's earnings have been skyrocketing, up 58% per annum for the past five years. Besterra is paying out less than half its earnings and cash flow, while simultaneously growing earnings per share at a rapid clip. This is a very favourable combination that can often lead to the dividend multiplying over the long term, if earnings grow and the company pays out a higher percentage of its earnings.

Many investors will assess a company's dividend performance by evaluating how much the dividend payments have changed over time. In the past eight years, Besterra has increased its dividend at approximately 9.1% a year on average. It's encouraging to see the company lifting dividends while earnings are growing, suggesting at least some corporate interest in rewarding shareholders.

To Sum It Up

From a dividend perspective, should investors buy or avoid Besterra? Besterra has been growing earnings at a rapid rate, and has a conservatively low payout ratio, implying that it is reinvesting heavily in its business; a sterling combination. There's a lot to like about Besterra, and we would prioritise taking a closer look at it.
